schools.summary: Abraham marries Keturah: 'then again Abraham took a wife, and her name was Keturah' — after Sarah's death (ch. 23) and Isaac's marriage (ch. 24), Abraham taking another wife, Keturah (the patriarch, still vigorous, though the marriage may, by some, have begun earlier, marrying Keturah, the sons of this marriage, vv.2-4, founding further nations, the Arabian tribes, the continuance of Abraham's house); Keturah later called a concubine (v.6; 1 Chronicles 1:32) — a secondary wife (the sons by her not sharing the inheritance with Isaac, vv.5-6, the marriage that brought forth the Keturahite/Arabian peoples, the wife distinguished from Sarah, the mother of the promised seed); Abraham's marriage to Keturah showing God's blessing on the patriarch (the promise that he should be a father of many nations, 17:4-5, further fulfilled, the founding of further peoples from Abraham, yet the covenant-line kept distinct in Isaac, vv.5-6) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Then again Abraham took a wife, and her name was Keturah
Abraham's marriage to Keturah
the secondary wife (v.6) whose sons would found further nations (17:4-5)
the covenant-line kept distinct in Isaac

schools.summary: The sons of Keturah: 'and she bare him Zimran, and Jokshan, and Medan, and Midian, and Ishbak, and Shuah' — Keturah bearing Abraham six sons, the heads of various Arabian tribes/peoples (Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, Shuah, the founding of further nations from Abraham, the Keturahite peoples of Arabia); among the sons, Midian (the father of the Midianites) the most notable in the later history (the Midianites, Exodus 2:15; Numbers 22; Judges 6-8, the heads of peoples, the spreading of Abraham's seed into the Arabian tribes); the six sons of Keturah heading various tribes of Arabia (the Keturahite Arabs, the fulfillment of the promise that Abraham should be a father of many nations, 17:4-5, the dividing of the peoples from Abraham, the covenant-line yet in Isaac) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And she bare him Zimran, and Jokshan, and Medan, and Midian, and Ishbak, and Shuah
the sons of Keturah
the heads of various Arabian tribes (Midian the most notable)
the fulfillment of the promise of many nations (17:4-5)

schools.summary: The descendants of Jokshan: 'and Jokshan begat Sheba, and Dedan. And the sons of Dedan were Asshurim, and Letushim, and Leummim' — the genealogy of Jokshan (Keturah's son): Sheba and Dedan, and Dedan's sons (Asshurim, Letushim, Leummim, the heads of further Arabian tribes, the spreading of the Keturahite line into peoples); Sheba and Dedan heads of Arabian peoples (the Sabeans and Dedanites, trading peoples of Arabia, mentioned later, Isaiah 21:13; Ezekiel 27:20, the tribes descended from Jokshan, the Keturahite Arabs); the sons of Dedan (Asshurim, Letushim, Leummim) — heads of further tribes or clans of Arabia (the dividing of the line into peoples, the genealogy tracing the Keturahite nations from Abraham) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Jokshan begat Sheba, and Dedan. And the sons of Dedan were Asshurim, and Letushim, and Leummim
the descendants of Jokshan
the heads of further Arabian tribes (the Sabeans and Dedanites, Isaiah 21:13)
the dividing of the Keturahite line into peoples

schools.summary: The sons of Midian, the close of Keturah's line: 'and the sons of Midian; Ephah, and Epher, and Hanoch, and Abida, and Eldaah. All these were the children of Keturah' — the sons of Midian (Keturah's son): Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, Eldaah (the heads of Midianite clans, the close of the genealogy of Keturah's children, the Keturahite peoples summed up); the sons of Midian heads of Midianite tribes (Ephah, mentioned Isaiah 60:6, the spreading of the Midianites, a people prominent in the later history, Numbers 22; Judges 6, the Keturahite Arabs); 'All these were the children of Keturah' (the summing of Keturah's descendants, vv.2-4, the founding of the Keturahite/Arabian peoples from Abraham, the fulfillment of the promise of many nations, 17:4-5, the line distinct from Isaac, the covenant-seed) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB.

themes: And the sons of Midian; Ephah, and Epher, and Hanoch, and Abida, and Eldaah. All these were the children of Keturah
the sons of Midian and the close of Keturah's line
the heads of Midianite clans (Isaiah 60:6), the Keturahite peoples summed
the line distinct from Isaac, the covenant-seed

schools.summary: Abraham gives all to Isaac: 'and Abraham gave all that he had unto Isaac' — Abraham making Isaac his sole heir, giving him all his substance (and the covenant-inheritance, the son of promise, 21:12, the inheritor of all, the distinguishing of Isaac from the sons of the concubines, v.6, the covenant-line and the inheritance settled on Isaac); Isaac receiving 'all' — the whole inheritance (the son of promise the heir, not the sons of Keturah or Hagar, the covenant-blessing and the substance settled on Isaac, the will of God, 21:12, in Isaac shall thy seed be called, carried out); Abraham's giving all to Isaac marking the narrowing of the covenant-line — Isaac alone the heir of the promise and the substance (the other sons provided for, but sent away, v.6, the line of promise kept in Isaac, and to be narrowed further to Jacob, vv.23, 33, the covenant carried forward) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Abraham gave all that he had unto Isaac
Abraham giving all to Isaac
the son of promise (21:12) the sole heir of substance and covenant
the narrowing of the covenant-line to Isaac

schools.summary: Abraham provides for and sends away the concubines' sons: 'but unto the sons of the concubines, which Abraham had, Abraham gave gifts, and sent them away from Isaac his son, while he yet lived, eastward, unto the east country' — to the sons of Keturah (and Hagar, the concubines, v.12), Abraham giving gifts (portions, provision, the patriarch's fairness, providing for all his sons, yet not making them heirs with Isaac, the distinguishing of the heir of promise from the other sons); Abraham 'sent them away from Isaac... while he yet lived, eastward, unto the east country' (Abraham, in his lifetime, sending the other sons away to the east, Arabia, that Isaac might dwell undisputed in the land of promise, the prudent separation, avoiding strife over the inheritance, the keeping of the covenant-line and the land in Isaac, the other sons founding the eastern, Arabian, peoples); 'eastward, unto the east country' (the sons of the concubines going east, to Arabia, the lands of the children of the east, Judges 6:3, the founding of the eastern peoples from Abraham, the dividing of the nations, the covenant-line kept distinct in Isaac in the land of promise) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: But unto the sons of the concubines... Abraham gave gifts, and sent them away from Isaac his son, while he yet lived, eastward, unto the east country
Abraham providing for and sending away the sons of the concubines
the patriarch providing for all, yet keeping the inheritance and land in Isaac
the other sons founding the eastern (Arabian) peoples (Judges 6:3)

schools.summary: The years of Abraham's life: 'and these are the days of the years of Abraham's life which he lived, an hundred threescore and fifteen years' — Abraham living a hundred and seventy-five years (the full age of the patriarch, a hundred years after his call at seventy-five, 12:4, seventy-five years after the promise, the long life of the father of the faithful, crowned with God's blessing, 24:1, the close of his pilgrimage); Abraham's hundred and seventy-five years — the long, blessed life of the patriarch (the fulfillment of the promise that he should die in a good old age, 15:15, the pilgrim's days numbered, the life of faith brought to its full term); the days of Abraham's life (175 years) marking the close of his earthly pilgrimage (Hebrews 11:13, the stranger and pilgrim's days fulfilled, the patriarch, having seen Isaac married and the covenant secured, ready to be gathered, v.8, the long life of faith) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And these are the days of the years of Abraham's life which he lived, an hundred threescore and fifteen years
the years of Abraham's life
the long, blessed life of the patriarch (12:4; 15:15)
the close of his earthly pilgrimage (Hebrews 11:13)

schools.summary: Abraham dies and is gathered to his people: 'then Abraham gave up the ghost, and died in a good old age, an old man, and full of years; and was gathered to his people' — the patriarch dying peacefully in a good old age, full of years (satisfied with life, the fulfillment of God's word, 15:15, thou shalt be buried in a good old age, the blessed, peaceful death of the father of the faithful, the close of his life); 'was gathered to his people' a phrase distinct from death and burial (he was gathered to his people before he was buried, v.9, and his 'people,' his fathers, were not buried in the same place, by the dominant reading the phrase intimating the survival of the soul and its gathering to the company of the departed, the immortal spirit gathered to his fathers in another state, Clarke noting the phrase points beyond the body to the soul, gathered to his people in the world of spirits, a hint, even in patriarchal times, of immortality and the life beyond, cf. Hebrews 11:13-16, the better country, some taking it more simply as a euphemism for death, joining the dead, the ancestors, but the distinction from burial supporting the fuller sense — the soul gathered to his kindred beyond); the 'gathering to his people' expressing the hope of the patriarchs — that death was not the end, but a gathering to the company of the faithful departed (the immortality of the soul intimated, Abraham gathered to his people, the faithful dead, in expectation of the resurrection and the better country, Hebrews 11:13-16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

debates.question: What does 'gathered to his people' mean?

sides.position: The survival of the soul and its gathering to the company of the faithful departed - distinct from death and burial (v.9), a hint of immortality and the better country (Hebrews 11:13-16)
A simpler euphemism for death - joining the dead, the ancestors

themes: Then Abraham gave up the ghost, and died in a good old age, an old man, and full of years; and was gathered to his people
Abraham's death and his being gathered to his people (the crux of the gathering)
the peaceful death in a good old age, full of years (15:15)
'gathered to his people' - the soul's survival and gathering to the faithful departed (Hebrews 11:13-16) vs a euphemism for death

schools.summary: Isaac and Ishmael bury Abraham: 'and his sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of Machpelah, in the field of Ephron the son of Zohar the Hittite, which is before Mamre' — Isaac and Ishmael, the two sons, together burying their father (the coming together of the brothers, Ishmael, cast out long before, ch. 21, here returning to honor his father, the burial of the patriarch in the tomb he had bought, ch. 23); the touching note — Isaac and Ishmael together at the burial (whatever the old estrangement, ch. 21, the brothers united in honoring their father, the reconciliation, or at least the coming together, at the grave, Ishmael acknowledging Isaac's precedence, named first); 'in the cave of Machpelah, in the field of Ephron... which is before Mamre' (Abraham buried in the cave he had purchased, ch. 23, where Sarah lay, 23:19, the patriarchal tomb, the burial in the land of promise, in the secure possession, the pledge of the inheritance and the resurrection, Hebrews 11:13-16) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And his sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of Machpelah... which is before Mamre
Isaac and Ishmael burying Abraham at Machpelah
the brothers united at the grave (whatever the old estrangement, ch. 21)
the burial in the purchased tomb where Sarah lay (ch. 23; 23:19)

schools.summary: Abraham buried with Sarah in the purchased field: 'the field which Abraham purchased of the sons of Heth: there was Abraham buried, and Sarah his wife' — Abraham buried in the field he had bought from the Hittites (ch. 23), where Sarah his wife already lay (23:19, the patriarchal tomb, the secure, purchased possession, the husband and wife reunited in the grave); the field Abraham's own (bought, ch. 23) — the secure possession (the first and only possession of the patriarch in the land of promise, a burying-place, the pledge of the future inheritance); 'there was Abraham buried, and Sarah his wife' (the patriarch laid with Sarah in the tomb, the burial in the land of promise, an act of faith and hope, Hebrews 11:13, the husband and wife together in the grave, awaiting the resurrection, the covenant-couple in the patriarchal tomb) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: The field which Abraham purchased of the sons of Heth: there was Abraham buried, and Sarah his wife
Abraham buried with Sarah in the purchased field
the secure, purchased possession in the land of promise (ch. 23)
the husband and wife reunited in the tomb, the pledge of the resurrection (Hebrews 11:13)

schools.summary: God blesses Isaac after Abraham's death: 'and it came to pass after the death of Abraham, that God blessed his son Isaac; and Isaac dwelt by the well Lahairoi' — after Abraham died, God blessing Isaac (the heir of the promise, the covenant-blessing passing to Isaac, God confirming to the son the blessing he had given the father, the promise continued, 26:3-4, the providence carrying the covenant forward); God blessing Isaac — the blessing of Abraham now resting on the son of promise (the continuity of the covenant, Isaac the heir of the blessing, and the substance, v.5, the providence over the covenant-line); 'Isaac dwelt by the well Lahairoi' (Isaac dwelling by the well of him that liveth and seeth me, 16:14; 24:62, the place associated with God's seeing and providence, the heir settled in the land of promise, the covenant-life continuing) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Wesley.

themes: And it came to pass after the death of Abraham, that God blessed his son Isaac; and Isaac dwelt by the well Lahairoi
God blessing Isaac after Abraham's death
the covenant-blessing passing to the son of promise (26:3-4)
the heir settled at the well of God's seeing (16:14; 24:62)

schools.summary: The generations of Ishmael introduced: 'now these are the generations of Ishmael, Abraham's son, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's handmaid, bare unto Abraham' — the genealogy of Ishmael (vv.12-18) given before that of Isaac (v.19), as the line of the non-elect son is recorded and dismissed before the covenant-line is traced (Ishmael, the son of Hagar, the Egyptian handmaid, 16:1, 15, Abraham's son, the record of the blessing on Ishmael, twelve princes, 17:20; v.16); Ishmael Abraham's son by Hagar (the Egyptian, Sarah's handmaid, 16:3) — the son of the bondwoman (Galatians 4:22-23, not the heir of promise, 21:10-12, yet blessed for Abraham's sake, 17:20, the line of the flesh recorded); the genealogy of Ishmael (the non-elect line) recorded first and briefly, then dismissed, that the narrative may proceed to Isaac (the covenant-line, v.19, the Scripture's pattern, as with Cain before Seth, Esau before Jacob, ch. 36, the blessing on Ishmael, the twelve princes, 17:20, noted, yet the covenant carried in Isaac) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Now these are the generations of Ishmael, Abraham's son, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's handmaid, bare unto Abraham
the generations of Ishmael introduced
the son of the bondwoman (16:1; Galatians 4:22), blessed for Abraham's sake (17:20)
the non-elect line recorded and dismissed before the covenant-line of Isaac (v.19)

schools.summary: The first of the sons of Ishmael: 'and these are the names of the sons of Ishmael, by their names, according to their generations: the firstborn of Ishmael, Nebajoth; and Kedar, and Adbeel, and Mibsam' — the sons of Ishmael, heads of the twelve tribes (v.16, Nebajoth, the firstborn, the Nabateans, and Kedar, a prominent Arabian tribe, Psalm 120:5; Isaiah 60:7, and others, the fulfillment of the promise of twelve princes, 17:20); Nebajoth and Kedar notable Arabian peoples (the Nabateans and the Kedarenes, the heads of Ishmaelite tribes, the spreading of Ishmael's line into the Arabian peoples); the sons of Ishmael (vv.13-15) twelve in number (v.16), heads of twelve tribes — the exact fulfillment of God's promise (17:20, twelve princes shall he beget, the blessing on Ishmael for Abraham's sake, the providence in the promise fulfilled) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And these are the names of the sons of Ishmael... the firstborn of Ishmael, Nebajoth; and Kedar, and Adbeel, and Mibsam
the first of the sons of Ishmael
the heads of Ishmaelite tribes (Nebajoth, Kedar; Isaiah 60:7)
the fulfillment of the promise of twelve princes (17:20)

schools.summary: More of the sons of Ishmael: 'and Mishma, and Dumah, and Massa' — more of the twelve sons of Ishmael (Mishma, Dumah, Massa, the heads of further Ishmaelite/Arabian tribes, the continuance of the list of the twelve princes, 17:20; v.16); Dumah (mentioned Isaiah 21:11) an Arabian tribe/place (the heads of peoples, the spreading of Ishmael's line, the Arabian tribes named); Mishma, Dumah, and Massa — heads of further clans of the Ishmaelites (the dividing of the line into tribes, the genealogy of the twelve princes, 17:20, continued, the Arabian peoples descended from Ishmael) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Mishma, and Dumah, and Massa
more of the sons of Ishmael
the heads of further Ishmaelite/Arabian tribes (Dumah, Isaiah 21:11)
the genealogy of the twelve princes (17:20) continued

schools.summary: The last of the sons of Ishmael: 'Hadar, and Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah' — the last of the twelve sons of Ishmael (Hadar, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah, the heads of the remaining Ishmaelite tribes, the completing of the twelve princes, 17:20; v.16, the Arabian peoples descended from Ishmael); Tema (mentioned Job 6:19; Isaiah 21:14) an Arabian tribe/place (the heads of peoples, the spreading of Ishmael's line, Jetur, the Itureans, the later Iturea, Luke 3:1, and Naphish also named); Hadar, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah completing the twelve sons of Ishmael (v.16) — the exact fulfillment of the promise (17:20, twelve princes, the heads of the twelve tribes of the Ishmaelites, the Arabian peoples from Abraham through Ishmael) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: Hadar, and Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah
the last of the sons of Ishmael
the heads of the remaining Ishmaelite tribes (Tema, Job 6:19; Jetur, Luke 3:1)
completing the twelve princes (17:20)

schools.summary: The twelve princes of Ishmael: 'these are the sons of Ishmael, and these are their names, by their towns, and by their castles; twelve princes according to their nations' — the twelve sons of Ishmael, settled in their towns and encampments (castles, fortified camps, the Ishmaelites dwelling in their tribal territories, the heads of the Arabian peoples, the spreading of Ishmael's line); the twelve sons 'twelve princes according to their nations' — the exact fulfillment of God's promise to Hagar and to Abraham (16:10; 17:20, twelve princes shall he beget, and I will make him a great nation, the blessing on Ishmael for Abraham's sake, the providence keeping the word, the twelve princes founding twelve tribes); the twelve princes of Ishmael a parallel, yet a contrast, to the twelve tribes of Israel, through Jacob, 35:22-26 (Ishmael blessed with a great nation, yet not the covenant-line, the temporal blessing on the son of the bondwoman, the covenant in Isaac) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB, Clarke.

themes: These are the sons of Ishmael... by their towns, and by their castles; twelve princes according to their nations
the twelve princes of Ishmael, the promise fulfilled
the exact fulfillment of God's word (16:10; 17:20), Ishmael blessed for Abraham's sake
a parallel, yet a contrast, to Israel's twelve tribes (35:22-26), the covenant in Isaac

schools.summary: The death of Ishmael: 'and these are the years of the life of Ishmael, an hundred and thirty and seven years: and he gave up the ghost and died; and was gathered unto his people' — Ishmael living a hundred and thirty-seven years and dying (the recording of his death, closing his genealogy, vv.12-18, the blessing of a long life on the son of the bondwoman, for Abraham's sake, 17:20); Ishmael's long life (137 years) — the temporal blessing on Abraham's son (the years of the son of the bondwoman, the close of his life); 'was gathered unto his people' (the same phrase used of Abraham, v.8, Ishmael, too, gathered to his people, his kindred/fathers, whether, by the fuller reading, a hint of the soul's survival, or a euphemism for death, the close of Ishmael's life, gathered like his father) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And these are the years of the life of Ishmael, an hundred and thirty and seven years: and he gave up the ghost and died; and was gathered unto his people
the death of Ishmael
the long life of the son of the bondwoman, for Abraham's sake (17:20)
'gathered unto his people' - the same phrase as of Abraham (v.8)

schools.summary: The dwelling-place of the Ishmaelites: 'and they dwelt from Havilah unto Shur, that is before Egypt, as thou goest toward Assyria: and he died in the presence of all his brethren' — the Ishmaelites dwelling over a wide region (from Havilah to Shur, the deserts between Egypt and Assyria, the Arabian peninsula and its borders, the spreading of Ishmael's tribes over Arabia, the fulfillment of the promise of a great nation, 17:20); the wide bounds of the Ishmaelites (Havilah to Shur, before Egypt, toward Assyria) — the Arabian peoples (the tribes ranging the deserts, the great nation of Ishmael spread abroad); 'he died in the presence of all his brethren' (or 'he settled/fell over against all his brethren') variously rendered: that Ishmael died (or his tribes settled, or fell, dwelt) in the presence of (over against, in defiance of, or among) all his brethren (the other descendants of Abraham, and his kindred, the fulfillment of the word that Ishmael would dwell 'in the presence of all his brethren,' 16:12, the wild man dwelling over against his kin, the Ishmaelites settled among/against their kindred) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: And they dwelt from Havilah unto Shur, that is before Egypt, as thou goest toward Assyria: and he died in the presence of all his brethren
the dwelling-place of the Ishmaelites
the tribes spread over Arabia, from Havilah to Shur
the fulfillment of the word that Ishmael would dwell over against his brethren (16:12)

schools.summary: The generations of Isaac introduced: 'and these are the generations of Isaac, Abraham's son: Abraham begat Isaac' — the genealogy of Isaac (the covenant-line) now begun, after the dismissal of Ishmael's line (vv.12-18, Isaac, Abraham's son, the son of promise, 21:12, the heir of the covenant, the narrative narrowing to the chosen line, the begetting of Isaac noted); the emphatic 'Abraham begat Isaac' — the direct, legitimate descent of the covenant-line (Isaac the true son and heir of Abraham, not Ishmael or the sons of Keturah, the line of promise traced from Abraham to Isaac, and to come, to Jacob); 'these are the generations of Isaac' (the history of Isaac's family, chiefly his sons Esau and Jacob, vv.21-34, the covenant-line carried in Isaac, the narrative tracing the chosen seed, through whom the promise, and the Messiah, would come, the generations of the son of promise) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And these are the generations of Isaac, Abraham's son: Abraham begat Isaac
the generations of Isaac introduced
the covenant-line carried in the son of promise (21:12)
the narrative narrowing to the chosen seed, through whom the Messiah would come

schools.summary: Isaac marries Rebekah at forty: 'and Isaac was forty years old when he took Rebekah to wife, the daughter of Bethuel the Syrian of Padanaram, the sister to Laban the Syrian' — Isaac marrying Rebekah at forty (three years after Sarah's death, 23:1; 24:67, Rebekah the daughter of Bethuel, the Syrian/Aramean of Padan-aram, and sister of Laban, the marriage of the heir, ch. 24, the covenant-wife of the kindred); Rebekah of Padan-aram (Mesopotamia), the daughter of Bethuel and sister of Laban (the kindred of Abraham, 22:20-23; 24:24, the wife of the kindred and faith, the covenant-line married within the family); 'the sister to Laban the Syrian' (Laban, who would figure largely in Jacob's history, chs. 29-31, named, the family connection, Rebekah of the Aramean kindred, the marriage that carried the covenant-line forward, the wife who would bear Esau and Jacob, vv.21-26) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Isaac was forty years old when he took Rebekah to wife, the daughter of Bethuel the Syrian of Padanaram, the sister to Laban the Syrian
Isaac's marriage to Rebekah at forty
Rebekah of the Aramean kindred of Abraham (22:20-23; 24:24)
the covenant-marriage within the kindred and faith

schools.summary: Isaac prays for the barren Rebekah, answered: 'and Isaac intreated the LORD for his wife, because she was barren: and the LORD was intreated of him, and Rebekah his wife conceived' — Rebekah barren (for nearly twenty years, vv.20, 26), Isaac praying earnestly to the LORD for her (the godly husband's prayer in the trial of barrenness, the dependence on God for the promised seed, the trial of faith, as with Sarah, and later Rachel and Hannah); Rebekah's barrenness — the trial of faith and patience (the seed of promise delayed, that it might be seen to be of God, not of nature, the occasion of Isaac's earnest prayer, the barrenness that magnified God's gift when the children came); 'the LORD was intreated of him, and Rebekah his wife conceived' (God hearing Isaac's prayer and giving conception, the answered prayer, the seed of promise given in answer to earnest entreaty, the providence over the covenant-line, the children, Esau and Jacob, a gift of God, sought in prayer) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Isaac intreated the LORD for his wife, because she was barren: and the LORD was intreated of him, and Rebekah his wife conceived
Isaac's prayer for the barren Rebekah, answered
the godly husband's earnest prayer in the trial of barrenness
the seed of promise given in answer to prayer, a gift of God

schools.summary: The children struggle, Rebekah inquires of the LORD: 'and the children struggled together within her; and she said, If it be so, why am I thus? And she went to enquire of the LORD' — the twins (Esau and Jacob) struggling (jostling, striving) within Rebekah's womb (the unusual, distressing movement, the prenatal striving that prefigured the future conflict of the two nations, and the two brothers, v.23, the sign of the struggle to come); Rebekah saying 'If it be so, why am I thus?' (perplexed and distressed at the unusual struggling, perhaps wondering why, having sought conception, she should suffer so, the troubled inquiry of the expectant mother, the perplexity that drove her to seek God); 'she went to enquire of the LORD' (Rebekah seeking the LORD, by prayer, or at some place/person of inquiry — perhaps Abraham yet living, or Melchizedek, or a prophetic word, to know the meaning of the struggle, the godly resort to God in perplexity, the seeking of God's mind, the inquiry answered by the oracle, v.23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And the children struggled together within her; and she said, If it be so, why am I thus? And she went to enquire of the LORD
the struggling of the children and Rebekah's inquiry of the LORD
the prenatal striving prefiguring the conflict of the two nations (v.23)
the perplexed mother's godly resort to God in her trouble

schools.summary: The oracle of the two nations - the elder shall serve the younger: 'and the LORD said unto her, Two nations are in thy womb, and two manner of people shall be separated from thy bowels; and the one people shall be stronger than the other people; and the elder shall serve the younger' — God revealing to Rebekah that her twins were the heads of two nations (Israel through Jacob, Edom through Esau, two peoples, divided in character and destiny, the prophecy explaining the struggle, v.22, the founding of the two nations); one people stronger than the other (the rivalry and conflict of the two nations, Israel and Edom, struggling through history, the dominance shifting); the great word — 'the elder shall serve the younger': contrary to nature and custom (the elder serving the younger, the birthright passing to Jacob), Esau (the elder) and his nation (Edom) would serve Jacob (the younger) and his nation (Israel), Paul citing this as the proof of God's sovereign election — 'the children being not yet born, neither having done any good or evil, that the purpose of God according to election might stand... it was said unto her, The elder shall serve the younger' (Romans 9:11-12; cf. Malachi 1:2-3, 'Jacob have I loved, but Esau have I hated'), the choice of Jacob over Esau, before they were born or had done good or evil, the foundation of the doctrine of unconditional election and the sovereignty of grace, God's free, sovereign choice of the covenant-line; the prophecy concerning both the two nations (Israel's later dominion over Edom, 2 Samuel 8:14) and the two persons (Jacob the elect heir, Esau the rejected) — Keil, Calvin, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'the elder shall serve the younger' establish?

sides.position: God's sovereign, unconditional election - the choice of Jacob over Esau before birth or works (Romans 9:11-13; Malachi 1:2-3), the foundation of the sovereignty of grace
The destiny of the two nations - Israel's later dominion over Edom (2 Samuel 8:14); the election viewed (also) of the peoples

themes: And the LORD said unto her, Two nations are in thy womb... and the elder shall serve the younger
the oracle of the two nations (the crux of election)
the elder shall serve the younger - God's sovereign choice of Jacob before birth or works (Romans 9:11-13; Malachi 1:2-3)
the two nations (Israel and Edom) and the election of persons, the sovereignty of grace

schools.summary: The birth of twins to Rebekah: 'and when her days to be delivered were fulfilled, behold, there were twins in her womb' — at the time of birth, Rebekah delivered of twins (Esau and Jacob, the fulfillment of the oracle, v.23, two nations in thy womb, the two sons born, the seed of promise given); the twins (Esau and Jacob) — the two nations now born (v.23, the answer to Isaac's prayer, v.21, the children of promise and conflict, the seed given of God); the birth of the twins fulfilling the oracle and the prayer (the two nations, Israel and Edom, springing from one womb, the providence over the covenant-line, the elder, Esau, and younger, Jacob, born, vv.25-26) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And when her days to be delivered were fulfilled, behold, there were twins in her womb
the birth of twins to Rebekah
the fulfillment of the oracle (v.23) and Isaac's prayer (v.21)
the two nations (Israel and Edom) springing from one womb

schools.summary: The birth and naming of Esau: 'and the first came out red, all over like an hairy garment; and they called his name Esau' — the firstborn twin coming out red (ruddy) and covered with hair (like a hairy garment, the unusual appearance, named Esau, 'hairy,' or by some 'made/finished,' the description marking his character, the hunter, the man of the field, v.27, and his nation, Edom, 'red,' v.30); the red and hairy appearance — tokens, by some, of his rough, wild nature (the hunter, v.27, the redness connected with 'Edom,' red, v.30, and the red pottage, v.30, the hairiness with 'Seir,' the hairy, the land of Edom, the marks of Esau's character and nation); 'they called his name Esau' (the firstborn named Esau, hairy, the elder son, yet to serve the younger, v.23, the father of Edom, v.30; ch. 36, the rough, profane man, v.34; Hebrews 12:16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And the first came out red, all over like an hairy garment; and they called his name Esau
the birth and naming of Esau
the ruddy, hairy firstborn, the marks of his nature and nation (Edom, v.30)
the elder son who would serve the younger (v.23)

schools.summary: The birth and naming of Jacob: 'and after that came his brother out, and his hand took hold on Esau's heel; and his name was called Jacob: and Isaac was threescore years old when she bare them' — the second twin coming out grasping Esau's heel, named Jacob ('heel-holder,' or 'supplanter,' the grasping a sign, a token of the future — Jacob taking hold of the birthright and blessing, vv.31-34; ch. 27, the name marking his character, the supplanter, as Esau would later say, 27:36); Jacob ('heel-catcher,' 'supplanter') named for the grasping of the heel — a prophetic token (the younger who would supplant the elder, vv.31-34; 27:36, the name later changed to Israel, 32:28, the covenant-heir); 'Isaac was threescore years old when she bare them' (Isaac sixty at the twins' birth, twenty years after his marriage at forty, v.20, the long barrenness, v.21, ended, the seed given after the trial of faith, the covenant-line continued) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And after that came his brother out, and his hand took hold on Esau's heel; and his name was called Jacob: and Isaac was threescore years old when she bare them
the birth and naming of Jacob
Jacob the heel-holder/supplanter (27:36), who would take the birthright and blessing
the twins born after the long trial of barrenness (v.21), Isaac being sixty

schools.summary: The contrast of Esau and Jacob: 'and the boys grew: and Esau was a cunning hunter, a man of the field; and Jacob was a plain man, dwelling in tents' — Esau growing to be a skilled hunter, a man of the open field (the wild, roving life, the rough, active outdoorsman, the man of the chase and the field, the character that suited the world and the flesh, the profane man, v.34; Hebrews 12:16); Jacob a 'plain' (Hebrew tam, perfect, upright, quiet, undefiled) man, dwelling in tents (the settled, domestic, contemplative life, 'plain' meaning sincere, upright, simple, not crafty, but honest and quiet, the godly, settled man, a keeper of flocks, a dweller in tents, given to the quieter, more spiritual life, the contrast with Esau's roving worldliness); the brothers differing — Esau the wild, worldly hunter, Jacob the plain, upright, quiet man of the tents (the contrast of the carnal and the comparatively godly, the elder suited to the world, the younger to the covenant-life, yet Jacob's own faults would appear, the two natures, fitting the two destinies, v.23) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And the boys grew: and Esau was a cunning hunter, a man of the field; and Jacob was a plain man, dwelling in tents
the contrast of Esau and Jacob
Esau the wild, worldly hunter; Jacob the plain (upright, quiet, tam) man of the tents
the carnal and the godly natures, fitting the two destinies (v.23)

schools.summary: The parental favoritism: 'and Isaac loved Esau, because he did eat of his venison: but Rebekah loved Jacob' — Isaac favoring Esau, in part because he ate of Esau's venison (the game Esau brought from his hunting, the father's partiality, swayed by the savory meat, and by Esau's firstborn place, the natural affection, and, alas, the weakness, of Isaac for the elder son); Isaac's love for Esau partly for the venison — a partiality swayed by appetite (the father's favoritism, a fault, the parents dividing in their affection, the love that would later be played upon, 27:4, the weakness of Isaac); 'but Rebekah loved Jacob' (Rebekah favoring Jacob, perhaps mindful of the oracle, v.23, that the elder should serve the younger, and drawn to the quiet, plain son, v.27, the parental favoritism divided, Isaac for Esau, Rebekah for Jacob, the partiality that would breed the deception of ch. 27, the household divided in affection, with its sad fruits) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Isaac loved Esau, because he did eat of his venison: but Rebekah loved Jacob
the parental favoritism
Isaac partial to Esau (swayed by the venison), Rebekah to Jacob (mindful of the oracle, v.23)
the divided household, the partiality that would breed the deception of ch. 27

schools.summary: Esau comes faint as Jacob cooks: 'and Jacob sod pottage: and Esau came from the field, and he was faint' — Jacob cooking (boiling) a pottage (of lentils, v.34), Esau coming in from the field (from his hunting), weary and faint (hungry, exhausted, the occasion of the selling of the birthright, vv.30-34, the everyday scene that became the trial of Esau's heart); Jacob boiling a pottage (a dish of red lentils, v.34) — the savory food (the occasion that Jacob would use to gain the birthright, v.31, the providence, and Jacob's seizing of the opportunity); 'Esau came from the field, and he was faint' (Esau, weary and famished from the hunt, coming craving food, the faintness, hunger, that would lead him to despise his birthright for a meal, v.34, the carnal man, ruled by appetite, the occasion of his profanity) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Jacob sod pottage: and Esau came from the field, and he was faint
Esau coming faint from the field as Jacob cooked
the weary, famished Esau craving food, the carnal man ruled by appetite
the scene that became the selling of the birthright (vv.30-34)

schools.summary: Esau craves the red pottage, whence Edom: 'and Esau said to Jacob, Feed me, I pray thee, with that same red pottage; for I am faint: therefore was his name called Edom' — Esau, famished, begging Jacob to feed him with the red pottage (the red lentil dish, v.34, the urgent craving of appetite, the carnal man, ruled by his hunger, the occasion of the bargain, v.31, 'that same red,' literally, 'that red, red'); the 'red' pottage — the red lentils (whence the name, the appetite fixed on the savory food, the craving that would cost Esau his birthright); 'therefore was his name called Edom' (Esau called Edom, 'red,' from the red pottage, and his own ruddiness, v.25, the name a perpetual memorial of his profane bargain, selling the birthright for a mess of red pottage, Edom the father of the Edomites, ch. 36, the name marking the man ruled by appetite) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Esau said to Jacob, Feed me, I pray thee, with that same red pottage; for I am faint: therefore was his name called Edom
Esau craving the red pottage, whence 'Edom'
the carnal man ruled by his hunger, the craving that would cost him his birthright
Edom (red), the name a memorial of his profane bargain

schools.summary: Jacob demands the birthright: 'and Jacob said, Sell me this day thy birthright' — Jacob, seizing the opportunity of Esau's hunger, demanding that Esau sell him the birthright (in exchange for the pottage, the bargain proposed, Jacob's eager, if not blameless, seeking of the birthright, which the oracle had assigned to him, v.23, yet he sought by his own contrivance); the birthright (bechorah) carrying many privileges — (1) the dignity and precedence (the headship of the family, the rule over the brethren); (2) a double portion of the paternal inheritance (Deuteronomy 21:17); (3) by some, the exercise of the priesthood (before its establishment in Aaron, the firstborn acting as priest, though this is questioned); and (4), supremely in this covenant family, the spiritual blessing — the line of the covenant and the promise (the Messianic line, the blessing of Abraham, the inheritance of Canaan and the covenant, the birthright thus joining temporal dignity with the spiritual, covenant import); in the family of Abraham the birthright chiefly spiritual — the covenant-blessing, the promise, the line of the Messiah (to sell it was to despise the spiritual inheritance, Jacob valuing it, though his means were faulty, Esau despising it, v.34) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

sides.position: Temporal privileges - the dignity/precedence (headship of the family), the double portion of inheritance (Deuteronomy 21:17), and (by some) the priesthood of the firstborn
Chiefly the spiritual/covenant import - the blessing of Abraham, the promise, the line of the Messiah (the spiritual inheritance despised by Esau)

themes: And Jacob said, Sell me this day thy birthright
Jacob's demand for the birthright (the crux of what the birthright was)
the temporal privileges (dignity, double portion, Deuteronomy 21:17, the priesthood) and the spiritual/covenant import
in this family chiefly the spiritual inheritance - the blessing of Abraham, the line of the Messiah

schools.summary: Esau's contempt for the birthright: 'and Esau said, Behold, I am at the point to die: and what profit shall this birthright do to me?' — Esau, ruled by his hunger, saying he was about to die (of faintness, an exaggeration of his craving), and asking what profit the birthright would be to him (if he died, the carnal reasoning that valued a present meal above the future, spiritual inheritance, the contempt for the birthright); Esau, faint with hunger, claiming to be at the point of death — the exaggeration of appetite (the present craving overruling all, the carnal man's reasoning, that a dying man has no use for a birthright, the excuse of the flesh); 'what profit shall this birthright do to me?' (Esau despising the birthright, counting it of no profit, compared to the present meal, the carnal estimate, valuing the temporal above the spiritual and eternal, the contempt that marked him a profane person, v.34; Hebrews 12:16, the despising of the covenant-blessing) — Gill.

themes: And Esau said, Behold, I am at the point to die: and what profit shall this birthright do to me?
Esau's contempt for the birthright
the carnal man, ruled by appetite, valuing a present meal above the spiritual inheritance
the contempt that marked him a profane person (v.34; Hebrews 12:16)

schools.summary: Esau sells the birthright by oath: 'and Jacob said, Swear to me this day; and he sware unto him: and he sold his birthright unto Jacob' — Jacob, to make the bargain sure, demanding an oath, Esau swearing (binding himself by oath) to sell the birthright (the contract sealed by oath, Jacob's care to secure the birthright, though his means were faulty, Esau's solemn, sworn surrender); Esau swearing — solemnly and irrevocably selling the birthright (the carnal man binding away his spiritual inheritance for a meal, the sworn bargain, the profane surrender of the covenant-blessing); 'he sold his birthright unto Jacob' (Esau selling, giving away, his birthright to Jacob for the pottage, the despising of the birthright in deed, v.34, the covenant-blessing passing to Jacob, as the oracle had said, v.23, the elder serving the younger, yet here by Esau's own profane act, the birthright surrendered for a mess of pottage, Hebrews 12:16) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Jacob said, Swear to me this day; and he sware unto him: and he sold his birthright unto Jacob
Esau selling the birthright by oath
the carnal man solemnly binding away his spiritual inheritance for a meal (Hebrews 12:16)
the covenant-blessing passing to Jacob (as the oracle had said, v.23)

schools.summary: Esau despises his birthright: 'and Jacob gave Esau bread and pottage of lentiles; and he did eat and drink, and rose up, and went his way: thus Esau despised his birthright' — Jacob giving Esau the bread and lentil pottage, Esau eating, drinking, rising, and going his way — carelessly, as if nothing of moment had passed (the indifference of the carnal man, having satisfied his appetite and thought no more of the birthright, the casual, unconcerned departure); the verdict of Scripture: Esau despised (held in contempt, made light of) his birthright (the profane estimate that valued a meal above the covenant-blessing and the spiritual inheritance, the Apostle calling Esau 'a profane person, who for one morsel of meat sold his birthright,' Hebrews 12:16, and warning against the like profanity, the despising of spiritual and eternal things for the carnal and temporal, the mark of the profane, worldly, godless, man, Esau's contempt the proof of his unworthiness of the covenant, and the justification, in his own conduct, of the election of Jacob, Romans 9, and the rejection of Esau, Malachi 1:2-3); Esau's despising his birthright a warning to all — that the profane, valuing the present and carnal above the spiritual and eternal, cast away their part in the world to come for a momentary gratification (the carnal wishes that make men despise the heavenly inheritance, the sin and folly of Esau a perpetual warning, Hebrews 12:16-17) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What does 'Esau despised his birthright' display?

sides.position: The mark of the profane person - the worldly, godless estimate valuing a meal above the covenant-blessing and the eternal inheritance (Hebrews 12:16); a warning against the like profanity
The proof, in Esau's own conduct, of his unworthiness - justifying the election of Jacob and the rejection of Esau (Romans 9; Malachi 1:2-3)

themes: And Jacob gave Esau bread and pottage of lentiles; and he did eat and drink, and rose up, and went his way: thus Esau despised his birthright
Esau despising his birthright (the crux of the profane person)
the profane estimate valuing a meal above the covenant-blessing and the eternal inheritance (Hebrews 12:16)
a warning against profanity, and the proof of Esau's unworthiness (Romans 9; Malachi 1:2-3)
